 {"id":"rich_rust-6dt","title":"Optimize String Allocation in Rendering","description":"## Objective
Reduce string allocations in the hot rendering path.

## Background
Rendering produces many small strings that are immediately discarded. This creates GC pressure and cache misses. Optimizations include buffer reuse and preallocated capacity.

## Areas to Optimize

### ANSI Code Generation
Current: Each style.render_to_ansi() allocates new String
Optimization: Pass mutable buffer to append to

```rust
// Before
fn render_to_ansi(&self) -> String {
    let mut result = String::new();
    // ...
    result
}

// After
fn render_to_ansi_into(&self, buf: &mut String) {
    buf.clear();
    // ...append directly...
}
```

### Segment Collection
Current: Vec<Segment> allocated for each renderable
Optimization: Use SmallVec or reuse buffers

### Text Building
Current: Many String concatenations
Optimization: Use String::with_capacity() with estimated size

### Console Output
Current: Build complete output then write
Optimization: Write directly to BufWriter

## Measurement
- Before/after benchmarks for each change
- Memory profiling (heaptrack or similar)

## Files to Modify
- src/style.rs
- src/segment.rs
- src/console.rs
- Cargo.toml (add smallvec if needed)","acceptance_criteria":"Measurable reduction in allocations.  {"id":"rich_rust-6os","title":"Implement LRU Cache for Color Parsing","description":"## Objective
Add LRU caching to color parsing as specified in RICH_SPEC.md Section 1.

## Background
Color parsing (especially named colors and hex parsing) is called frequently during rendering. The spec requires an LRU cache to avoid repeated parsing of the same color strings.

## Technical Details

### Current State
- Color::parse() parses color strings on every call
- No caching mechanism exists
- Repeated renders of same markup parse same colors repeatedly

### Required Implementation
1. Add LRU cache (suggest using lru crate or std HashMap with eviction)
2. Cache key: color string (e.g., 'red', '#ff0000', 'rgb(255,0,0)')
3. Cache value: parsed Color enum variant
4. Cache size: configurable, default 256 entries

### Implementation Approach
```rust
use std::sync::Mutex;
use lru::LruCache;

lazy_static! {
    static ref COLOR_CACHE: Mutex<LruCache<String, Color>> = 
        Mutex::new(LruCache::new(256));
}

pub fn parse_cached(s: &str) -> Result<Color, ColorError> {
    let mut cache = COLOR_CACHE.lock().unwrap();
    if let Some(color) = cache.get(s) {
        return Ok(color.clone());
    }
    let color = Color::parse(s)?;
    cache.put(s.to_string(), color.clone());
    Ok(color)
}
```

### Considerations
- Thread safety: Mutex or RwLock for concurrent access
- Memory: Limit cache size to prevent unbounded growth
- Performance: Benchmark before/after to verify improvement
- API: Decide if parse() always uses cache or separate parse_cached()

## Files to Modify
- src/color.rs (add caching)
- Cargo.toml (add lru dependency if needed)

## Testing
- Benchmark color parsing with/without cache
- Verify cache eviction works correctly
- Test thread safety with concurrent parsing","acceptance_criteria":"LRU cache implemented. Benchmarks show performance improvement. Thread-safe.","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","owner":"jeff141421@gmail.com","created_at":"2026-01-17T10:20:41.980918356-05:00","created_by":"Dicklesworthstone","updated_at":"2026-01-18T02:07:25.737404139-05:00","closed_at":"2026-01-18T02:07:25.737404139-05:00","close_reason":"LRU cache for color parsing fully implemented in src/color.rs. Uses LazyLock<Mutex<LruCache<String, Color>>> with 1024 entries. Thread-safe with mutex. Normalizes inputs via trim+lowercase. parse() checks cache first, calls parse_uncached() on miss, then caches result.","labels":["implementation","performance"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"rich_rust-6os","depends_on_id":"rich_rust-a96","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-17T10:22:52.303872769-05:00","created_by":"Dicklesworthstone"},{"issue_id":"rich_rust-6os","depends_on_id":"rich_rust-yz6","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-17T10:25:08.549524038-05:00","created_by":"Dicklesworthstone"},{"issue_id":"rich_rust-6os","depends_on_id":"rich_rust-g92","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-17T10:43:22.740309401-05:00","created_by":"Dicklesworthstone"}]}

### 9.4 Width Calculation Algorithm (CRITICAL)
- [ ] Verify get_row_widths() measures each cell
- [ ] Verify calculate_widths() implements the algorithm:

  2. If total < available, expand_widths()
  3. If total > available, collapse_widths()
- [ ] Verify expand_widths() distributes extra space by ratio
- [ ] Verify collapse_widths() reduces widths proportionally

### 9.5 Row Rendering

- [ ] Verify multi-line cells are handled

### 9.6 Table Rendering

- [ ] Verify row separators when show_lines=true
- [ ] Verify caption appears below table
- [ ] Verify expand=true fills available width

### 9.7 Edge Cases
- [ ] Test empty table
- [ ] Test single column
- [ ] Test very narrow width
- [ ] Test cells with wide characters (CJK)

## Files to Examine

- src/measure.rs (Measurement)

 {"id":"rich_rust-85g","title":"Verify Hyperlink (OSC 8) Support","description":"## Objective
Verify and complete hyperlink support using OSC 8 escape sequences.

## Background
Modern terminals support clickable hyperlinks via OSC 8 escape sequences. The Style struct has a link field, but rendering may be incomplete.

## Technical Details

### OSC 8 Format
```
ESC ] 8 ; params ; URI ST ... text ... ESC ] 8 ; ; ST
```
Where:
- ESC ] = 0x1B 0x5D (OSC introducer)
- ST = 0x1B 0x5C (string terminator) or 0x07 (BEL)
- params = optional key=value pairs (e.g., id=unique-id)
- URI = the link URL

### Example
```
\x1b]8;;https://example.com\x07Click here\x1b]8;;\x07
```

### Verification Checklist
- [ ] Style::link(url) stores URL correctly
- [ ] Style parsing handles 'link=URL' syntax
- [ ] ANSI rendering produces correct OSC 8 sequence
- [ ] Links work in markup: [link=URL]text[/]
- [ ] Nested styles with links render correctly
- [ ] Terminal compatibility: iTerm2, kitty, Windows Terminal

### Implementation Gaps (if found)
- May need to add OSC 8 rendering to style.rs render_to_ansi()
- May need console.rs to check terminal hyperlink support

## Files to Examine
- src/style.rs (link field, ANSI rendering)
- src/console.rs (terminal capability detection)
- RICH_SPEC.md Section 2.2","acceptance_criteria":"Hyperlinks render correctly.  {"id":"rich_rust-b7s","title":"EPIC: End-to-End Testing & Validation Infrastructure","description":"## Objective
Create comprehensive end-to-end test infrastructure with detailed logging to ensure rich_rust works correctly as a complete system.

## Background
Unit tests verify individual components, but E2E tests verify the entire rendering pipeline works correctly from user input to terminal output. This is critical for a terminal rendering library where visual correctness matters.

## Why E2E Tests Are Critical
1. **Integration verification**: Components that pass unit tests may fail when combined
2. **Visual regression detection**: Catch rendering changes that affect appearance
3. **Performance regression detection**: Catch slowdowns in real-world scenarios
4. **Cross-platform validation**: Ensure consistent behavior across environments

## Scope

### Test Infrastructure
- Test harness with detailed logging (using tracing crate)
- Golden file comparison for visual output
- Performance regression detection
- CI integration with artifact preservation

### Test Scenarios
- Complete rendering pipeline tests
- Complex document rendering
- Edge case combinations
- Performance benchmarks as tests

### Logging Requirements
- Structured logging with tracing
- Test-specific log levels
- Timing information for each phase
- Memory usage tracking
- Diff output for failures

## Success Criteria
- All major user scenarios covered by E2E tests
- Detailed logs available for debugging failures
- CI runs tests and preserves artifacts
- Performance baselines established and monitored","acceptance_criteria":"E2E test suite operational. All scenarios pass. Logs are detailed and useful.","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"epic","owner":"jeff141421@gmail.com","created_at":"2026-01-17T10:43:43.32014929-05:00","created_by":"Dicklesworthstone","updated_at":"2026-01-18T01:56:36.803801957-05:00","closed_at":"2026-01-18T01:56:36.803801957-05:00","close_reason":"E2E testing infrastructure complete: comprehensive regression tests (35), golden file tests (37), performance baseline tests (20), logging infrastructure, and CI artifact preservation for debugging"}
-{"id":"rich_rust-bj1","title":"Implement Triple-Duty Test Framework","description":"## Overview
Apply the 'triple duty' testing concept from beads_rust to rich_rust. This approach makes integration tests serve three simultaneous purposes:

## The Three Duties

### 1. Integration Tests
- Exhaustive end-to-end integration tests exercising all public APIs
- Cover edge cases, error conditions, and typical use patterns
- Run with `cargo test`

### 2. Conformance Harness
- Compare behavior against Python Rich (the original implementation)
- Generate test outputs and compare with Python Rich's outputs
- Document any intentional behavioral differences
- Useful for ensuring compatibility when porting features

### 3. Benchmark Harness
- Same tests used as realistic benchmarks
- Measure render performance, memory usage
- Compare against Python Rich performance
- Track performance regressions over time

## Implementation Ideas

1. Create a `tests/conformance/` directory with tests that:
   - Generate output from rich_rust
   - Compare against expected outputs (potentially generated from Python Rich)
   
2. Create a `benches/` directory with criterion benchmarks that:
   - Reuse the same test scenarios
   - Measure render_to_string performance
   - Track segment count and allocation overhead

3. Consider a test harness macro that allows the same test case to:
   - Run as a normal test
   - Run as a conformance check
   - Run as a benchmark

## Benefits
- Maximum test coverage with minimal code duplication
- Ensures Python Rich compatibility
- Catches performance regressions automatically
- Professional-grade testing infrastructure","status":"in_progress","priority":2,"issue_type":"feature","owner":"jeff141421@gmail.com","created_at":"2026-01-18T21:02:26.451406501-05:00","created_by":"Dicklesworthstone","updated_at":"2026-01-19T02:10:13.757579519Z"}

 {"id":"rich_rust-de4","title":"Audit Error Handling Consistency","description":"## Objective
Audit all error handling to ensure consistency and helpful error messages.

## Background
Good error messages are critical for library usability. This task audits all error types and messages for consistency and helpfulness.

## Audit Checklist

### Error Types
- [ ] All modules use consistent error type pattern (thiserror recommended)
- [ ] Error types implement std::error::Error
- [ ] Error types implement Display with helpful messages
- [ ] Error types implement Debug

### Error Messages
- [ ] Messages explain WHAT went wrong
- [ ] Messages explain WHY it's an error
- [ ] Messages suggest HOW to fix (where applicable)
- [ ] No raw "unwrap failed" or "unexpected error"

### Example Good vs Bad
```rust
// BAD
Err(ColorError::InvalidColor)

// GOOD
Err(ColorError::InvalidColorName {
    name: name.to_string(),
    suggestion: Self::suggest_similar_color(name),
    help: "Valid colors are: red, green, blue, ... or use #RRGGBB hex format",
})
```

### Audit by Module
- [ ] color.rs - ColorError
- [ ] style.rs - StyleError
- [ ] markup/mod.rs - MarkupError
- [ ] text.rs - TextError (if any)
- [ ] console.rs - ConsoleError (if any)
- [ ] renderables/table.rs - TableError (if any)

### Recovery Behavior
- [ ] Markup parser: graceful degradation for invalid tags
- [ ] Color parser: clear error for invalid colors
- [ ] Style parser: clear error for invalid attributes
- [ ] Table: handle edge cases without panic

### Testing Error Paths
- [ ] All error variants have tests
- [ ] Error messages verified in tests
- [ ] Recovery behavior documented

## Files to Examine
- All files with Error types
- All uses of unwrap(), expect(), panic!()

## Deliverables
1. Audit report of current state
2. List of improvements needed
3. Consistency guidelines document","acceptance_criteria":"All error handling audited. Improvements documented.  {"id":"rich_rust-g92","title":"Validate Color System against RICH_SPEC Section 1","description":"# Validate Color System against RICH_SPEC Section 1

## Context
The Color System is foundational to rich_rust. It handles color representation, parsing, palette management, and ANSI code generation.

## Spec Requirements to Validate (from RICH_SPEC.md §1)

### 1.1 Data Structures
- [ ] ColorTriplet: `hex()`, `rgb()`, `normalized()` methods
- [ ] ColorSystem enum: STANDARD (1), EIGHT_BIT (2), TRUECOLOR (3), WINDOWS (4)
- [ ] ColorType enum: DEFAULT (0), STANDARD (1), EIGHT_BIT (2), TRUECOLOR (3), WINDOWS (4)
- [ ] Color struct: name, color_type, number, triplet fields

### 1.2 Color Parsing (CRITICAL)
- [ ] Named colors: "red", "bright_blue" → STANDARD/EIGHT_BIT
- [ ] Hex format: "#FF0000" → TRUECOLOR
- [ ] Color number: "color(196)" → STANDARD if 0-15, EIGHT_BIT if 16-255
- [ ] RGB format: "rgb(255,0,0)" → TRUECOLOR
- [ ] Default: "default" → ColorType::DEFAULT
- [ ] Case-insensitivity
- [ ] Whitespace handling in rgb()
- [ ] **LRU cache (max 1024 entries)** - verify caching exists

### 1.3 Color Palettes
- [ ] STANDARD_PALETTE: 16 colors with exact RGB values
- [ ] EIGHT_BIT_PALETTE: 256 colors (16 standard + 216 cube + 24 grayscale)
- [ ] WINDOWS_PALETTE: 16 colors with exact RGB values
- [ ] 6x6x6 cube formula: `16 + 36 * red_index + 6 * green_index + blue_index`
- [ ] Grayscale ramp: indices 232-255 with values (8,8,8) to (238,238,238)

### 1.4 Color Conversion Algorithms
- [ ] RGB to 8-bit: grayscale detection via HLS saturation < 0.15
- [ ] RGB to Standard: weighted CIE76 color distance formula
- [ ] Verify exact formulas match spec

### 1.5 ANSI Code Generation
- [ ] DEFAULT: ["39"] foreground, ["49"] background
- [ ] STANDARD 0-7: ["30"+n] fg, ["40"+n] bg
- [ ] STANDARD 8-15: ["82"+n] fg, ["92"+n] bg
- [ ] EIGHT_BIT: ["38", "5", "N"] fg, ["48", "5", "N"] bg
- [ ] TRUECOLOR: ["38", "2", "R", "G", "B"] fg, ["48", "2", "R", "G", "B"] bg

## Files to Check
- `src/color.rs` (955 lines)

## Validation Approach
1. Read src/color.rs thoroughly
2. Compare each implementation detail against spec
3. Create tests for any uncovered edge cases
4. Document discrepancies
5. Fix implementation or update spec as needed

- Color conversion algorithms match exactly
- ANSI code generation matches spec table","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","owner":"jeff141421@gmail.com","created_at":"2026-01-17T10:13:47.105219826-05:00","created_by":"Dicklesworthstone","updated_at":"2026-01-17T23:34:03.392808606-05:00","closed_at":"2026-01-17T23:34:03.392811762-05:00","close_reason":"Color System validation complete - all RICH_SPEC Section 1 requirements verified:

 {"id":"rich_rust-h51","title":"Validate Ratio Distribution against RICH_SPEC Section 14","description":"## Objective
Verify the ratio distribution algorithm matches RICH_SPEC.md Section 14 exactly.

## Background
Ratio distribution is used by Table to allocate extra/reduced space among columns. It's a weighted distribution algorithm that must handle edge cases like zero ratios and integer rounding.

## Validation Checklist

### 14.1 Basic Algorithm
- [ ] Verify ratio_distribute(total, ratios) function signature
- [ ] Verify distribution proportional to ratios
- [ ] Verify sum of results equals total exactly (no loss/gain)

### 14.2 Distribution Logic
- [ ] Test: distribute(100, [1,1,1]) = [34,33,33] or similar (rounding)

 {"id":"rich_rust-ms5","title":"Verify Table Width Collapse Algorithm","description":"## Objective
Verify collapse_widths() in Table matches RICH_SPEC.md Section 9 exactly.

## Background
When table content exceeds available width, collapse_widths() must reduce column widths proportionally while respecting minimum widths. This is a complex algorithm with many edge cases.

## Technical Details

### Spec Requirement (Section 9.4)
collapse_widths() should:
1. Calculate excess = current_total - available_width
2. Distribute reduction proportionally to (current - minimum)
3. Never reduce below minimum width
4. Handle columns that hit minimum (redistribute to others)

### Algorithm Verification
```
Given: 3 columns with widths [40, 30, 30] = 100
       minimums [10, 10, 10]
       available width = 70
       excess = 30

Step 1: Calculate reducible space per column
  col0: 40-10 = 30 reducible
  col1: 30-10 = 20 reducible
  col2: 30-10 = 20 reducible
  total reducible = 70

Step 2: Distribute reduction proportionally
  col0 reduces by: 30 * (30/70) = ~12.9
  col1 reduces by: 30 * (20/70) = ~8.6
  col2 reduces by: 30 * (20/70) = ~8.6

Result: [27, 21, 22] (with rounding)
```

### Test Cases
1. All columns have same reducible space
2. One column at minimum (can't reduce further)
3. All columns at minimum
4. Single column table
5. Very narrow width (forces all to minimum)

## Files to Examine
- src/renderables/table.rs (collapse_widths function)

Create a migration guide helping Python Rich users adopt rich_rust.

## Guide Contents

### Feature Mapping Table
| Python Rich | rich_rust | Notes |
|-------------|-----------|-------|
| console.print() | Console::print() | Same semantics |
| Text() | Text::new() | Similar API |
| Style() | Style::new() | Builder pattern |
| Table() | Table::new() | Similar API |
| Panel() | Panel::new() | Similar API |
| Markdown() | Markdown::parse() | Feature flag |
| Syntax() | Syntax::new() | Feature flag |
| Live() | Not implemented | Excluded |
| Status() | Not implemented | Excluded |

### API Differences
- Python: console.print(text, style='bold')
- Rust: console.print('[bold]text[/]');

### Markup Syntax
- Same: [bold], [red], [on blue], [/]
- Same: Escaping with backslash

### Console Options
- force_terminal -> force_terminal
- record -> record
- width -> width()
- color_system -> color_system()

### Common Patterns

Python:
```python
console = Console()
table = Table(title='My Table')
table.add_column('Name')
table.add_row('Alice')
console.print(table)
```

Rust:
```rust
let console = Console::new();
let table = Table::new()
    .title('My Table')
    .add_column(Column::new('Name'))
    .add_row(Row::new(vec\![Cell::new('Alice')]));
console.print(table);
```

### Feature Flags
- syntax: Syntax highlighting (requires syntect)
- markdown: Markdown rendering (requires pulldown-cmark)
- json: JSON rendering (requires serde_json)
